BRENT LAB

Reviews and commentary about biological function by Roger Brent

  • Blog

Preparing to atone, Part 3: The decision not to publish (wonkish)

April 17, 2018 by Connor Woolworth

We had wanted to publish these results on information carrying capacity as part of a larger work. In 2006, we had submitted another part of this story for publication as an article to Nature. The title of that 2006 manuscript was “Signal propagation and establishment in yeast pheromone response system requires fast-acting negative feedback”. In that work we followed the signal down the various steps in the path, and found a fast acting negative feedback carried out by a MAP Kinase, Fus3. For our 2006 submission, Nature’s editorial decision was negative, with the summary being the boilerplate “While the reviewers find your work of some interest, concerns are raised about the advance your findings represent over earlier work and the strength of the novel conclusions that can be drawn at this stage.”

We found this unfair, and told the Nature editors so, and, from our correspondence drew the inference that, if we came back to Nature with findings that were clearly an advance over previous work, including a measurement of the system’s ability to transmit information, we might be able to publish that.

Our results on information capacity informed our 2007 manuscript, whose new title, reflecting our information transmission experiments, was “Negative feedback optimizes information transmission in a cell signaling system”. Reviews of this manuscript were mixed, but we believed that the door to publication was open if we addressed the reviewer’s comments. So we did.

But, in Summer 2008, as we prepared again to submit the article, we left out the measurements of channel information carrying capacity. In addition to uncertainty as to the best way to define the input dose distribution (as I described in a previous post) there were three main reasons for not including the channel capacity calculation.

One reason was dispute about the interpretation to place on the response shift experiment. This was the subject of vigorous discussion within the group. At the low ligand concentrations, there were perfectly sensible mechanistic explanations for the increase in noise in transmitted signal that did not require use of the words information or entropy. We had measured the number of molecules of the Ste2 receptor per cell at about 6,600 (Thomson et al., 2011, Table S1) and our sensitized Δsst2 strain gave robust responses with only single digit numbers of the receptor bound. The increased cell-cell variation in transmitted signal in that Δsst2 cells at low ligand concentrations could be due to stochastic variation in low-integer numbers of the Ste2 receptor bound.

Given that in molecular-cell-biological terms, we had a perfectly satisfying mechanistic explanation for degradation of information transmission when we perturbed the system to make the dose response more sensitive, why invoke the more abstract concept of “mutual information” and “channel capacity”?

And if we were not emphasizing these concepts, why publish the measurement?

The second was, in retrospect, at least a little craven. We were by then in a hurry. We had been trying to publish this work for almost two years and we had an opportunity to publish it in Nature as we believe it deserved to be published. We had pending grant applications whose fortunes would be improved by publishing the key work. Why risk any further complication by tackling this seemingly less important issue?

The third was that the starting idea that I had pushed was fundamentally incorrect. I had thought we could distribution of reporter gene outputs collection of induced single cells as the “output distribution” in a mutual information calculation. This was worse than naive. We knew better because we had previously identified and quantified sources of cell-to-cell variation in the response (Colman-Lerner et al, 2005).

And we knew that variation was large. For even one such source, the difference in ability of cells to express genes into proteins we quantified by the variable G, the top 5% of the cells in the population expressed 6 times as much reporter output as the cells in the bottom 5%.

So that the distribution of outputs in a population of cells at any give dose would be broad. Individual cells respond much more precisely than the swarm defined by the population’s output distribution.

Due to the cell-to-cell variability, our use of the uncorrected output distribution of the population as a measure of how precisely a single cell had responded was therefore…. idiotic.

The mutual information we calculated by such means would only only be a lower bound.

True, the same careful measurements of cell-to-cell variation offered us a way to correct (at least in part) for the effects of cell-to-cell variation on the output distribution, and thus to gain a better measure, a higher lower bound, for the amount of information carried by the signaling channel in individual cells.

But rather than publish a lower bound number with these caveats, some of the key players here, certainly including me opted to reinforce a pretty elitist self image: super careful, super pure, kind of like so many Arthurian knights….

Sans [some mortal] peur et sans reproche.

This was at least self-important, if not stuck-up.  And it had negative consequences.

References

 Colman-Lerner, A., Gordon, A., Serra, E., Chin, T., Resnekov, O., Endy, D., Pesce, G. and Brent, R. (2005) Regulated cell-to-cell variation in a cell fate decision system. Nature. 437, 699-706 https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16170311

Thomson, T. M., Benjamin, K. R., Thomson, T. M., Love, T., Yu, R., Gordon, A., Colman-Lerner, A., Endy, D. and Brent, R. (2011) Scaffold number in yeast signaling system sets tradeoff between system output and dynamic range. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. USA, 108, 20265-20270. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22114196

April 17, 2018 /Connor Woolworth
